sentences.text: Oh, that's in 1944.
Yes.
Yes.
Yeah.
We used to get letters from him.
Yes.
And until, until, you know-- until-- we were still home, you know?
And then afterwards, of course, I never heard from him again.
Never saw him again.
He may have-- I'm not really sure, but he may have come home one time furlough.
I don't remember for sure.
I, I-- it seems to me like he did, maybe one time.
Yes.
And, and that was it.
Yes.
So anyway, so from Birkenau, they made up a transport to go to Warsaw.
Well, it-- very cramped in, very cramped in.
And I knew-- I knew that, you know, they were talking about there were also gypsies there.
That there were Jews and gypsies in there.
And I-- they, they were talking about, you know, but I don't remember exactly.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] A lot of-- a lot of us, you know, a lot of the Jews are being cremated, being killed in there, you know?
Yes.
That's what, you know, I heard about.
I heard.
No.
I didn't see it.
I didn't see it.
I don't remember.
I really don't remember too much about Birkenau.
I really don't.
But in Warsaw-- we arrived in Warsaw.
And we get off the train, and I look around.
As far as the eye can see, all around, not a one building standing.
Everything was leveled.
That used to be the Warsaw Ghetto.
So I said to myself, I said, my goodness.
Well, where we are we going to go?
Where are we going to be-- where is going to be our headquarters?
Where sleeping?
Where are we going to be sleeping?
So we march, and march, and march.
And finally, we got to the camp.
There was already a camp, a concentration camp, built up.
In the same place where the ghetto used to be?
Yes, it was still in the ghetto, yes.
And there were people from Greece, Holland, France, and Polish.
Yeah.
And, and then-- and then our group came from our part of the country.
Yes.
Yes.
Well, we-- I don't remember.
Well, I think we were in the same barrack.
Oh.
Well, when we got out of the train, like I was saying, that, you know, I couldn't see not even a single building standing.
But as we kept on going, we finally, we got to the camp.
Oh.
We were wearing this [? halfling ?] clothes.
You know, like prison clothes.
Stripes.
Blue.
Blue and white.
Yes.
Yeah.
When we got there, they shaved our hair.
To Birkenau.
Yes.
And then later on--
Pardon me?
I don't remember if I still wore-- I don't remember whether they gave me shoes or what.
I don't remember.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But then, in Warsaw, when the hair started to grow, what they did is they put the-- made-- what do you call it?
They made a line in the middle, so it always it looks different.
There's a line in the middle.
They cut off the hair, just right in the middle.
Yeah.
There is a space,
As sort of identification, I suppose.
Yeah.
Well, they, they had a special barber.
Yeah.
I had a number only on my jacket.
So when I got to Warsaw, I got a number.
And we used to go out and work in the ghetto to clean up, clean up the ghetto.
And if there's some bricks, to clean off the brick from the cement and save the bricks.
And, and that was our job.
Yes.
Yeah.
Well, I guess he just had to cope with it.
You know, do the best you can.
And then me, too.
And all of us.
Yes.
Very little.
Very little.
They gave us like a cup of coffee in the morning, and lunch time was a bowl of soup.
And at dinner time was a bowl of soup with a piece of bread.
And you had a choice to eat it for dinner or to save it in the morning with a cup of coffee.
And, and if you saved it in the morning, there was a very good chance that piece of bread is gone.
Somebody would steal it from you.
Well, I guess, I used to eat it.
Straightaway.
Yeah.
Well, the, the Greek people-- they were Jews also, you know?
The Greeks, they were, by nature, happy people.
And they were always singing.
When we marched-- when we marched to work, they were singing their Greek songs.
And they were try to be happy.
Yes, I suppose I, I picked up a few songs, they also-- but I don't remember now, you know?
But I used to-- and they also sang French songs.
But it was-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] so anyway, they were trying to keep the morale up.
That's what it was.
And I remember one time, we were marching to work.
And it was pouring rain.
And I started to cry.
So the man next to me said, look here, little boy, if you want to stay alive, you can't cry.
You have to be strong.
And you know, you have to think that some day, you're going to be free again.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
So I always remember that.
And that kept me strong.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] Then one day, I came down with the typhoid fever.
And I'm at work.
And I just couldn't work good, like I used to.
So the kapo came over.
And he say, what's wrong with you?
You're slow.
And he looked at me.
He said-- I was flushed.
My face was flushed.
He said, when we go home, after you eat, you go to the doctor.
And if I see you here tomorrow and you didn't go to the doctor, you're in big trouble.
I did.
I had no other choice.
I went, and they put me in the hospital immediately.
And I don't even know how long I was there.
Most of the time, I was just sleeping.
Sleeping, and I guess I ate whatever they gave me, and sleeping.
And then when I came out, I think it was the following day, they had a roll call.
Everybody out.
And they gave us a lecture.
They say we have to leave from here.
And we have a few boxcars for those people that cannot walk.
The rest of you are going to have to walk.
Now all those that feel that they cannot walk should step aside, and they will provide them with transportation.
So they used to call them blockalteste, the one that was in charge of the barracks.
So he came over to me.
He said, you just came out of the hospital.
You think you'll be able to walk or do you want to go by train?
Yes, sir.
I'll be able to walk.
I feel strong.
[PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS]
Then, we-- I think it was the following day.
We started to march.
We were-- started to march.
And me, my brother, and my father, we tried to stick together.
But the Nazis, when they saw somebody trying to stick together, they did everything possible-- they used to beat us-- to keep us apart.
So we, we couldn't be hold together.
We lost each other.
So I was separate.
My brother was separate.
My father was separate.
And we, we marched as far as Kutno, which is close to the German border.
And there, we were like in the forest over there, waiting for the train.
We were there for approximately about three days.
And the train-- we got there before the train came.
And it was pouring, raining like cats and dogs.
I don't know why they say cats and dogs, but it was raining very heavily.
And I, I remember I laid on one side, and I didn't want to turn over on the other side because here it's dryer.
It's wet, but it's-- you know, I'm used to.
But if I turn over, then I'm going to feel the wetness more.
So I was just, most of the time, on one side, as far as I can remember.
Maybe I did move over.
I don't remember.
But I just keep on to lay on that one side for a long time.
Then when the train came-- the doors are in the middle.
So they put like 60 people on one side.
You used to sit down, spread your legs, and another person sat down in between.
And that went row by row.
You couldn't-- you couldn't get up for nothing.
And the train took us to Dachau.
We arrived in Dachau, and we were very undernourished.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So they still needed us for labor.
So they gave us a little bit of water at a time and a little bit of food at a time, so we don't get sick and die.
And they put us back on our feet.
And then they shipped us to a place called Muhldorf.
That's in Bayern.
Just, just myself.
I lost the others, yes.
No.
No, not until after the war, when I met my brother on the way home.
So we-- they shipped us from, from Dachau to Muhldorf.
It was in the forest.
That's why they called it the Waldlager.
We used to even cut down trees and then put up these pre-fabricated barracks.
And, and then they used to send us out to work carrying cement.
They just put a sack of cement on the back and you carried it.
And what I heard from other people-- what they were building is an underground airfield.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And that was in the summertime.
For the winter, they, they built another camp a little further down, which was bunkers.
It was more like-- half of it was underground and the other half has a roof on top of it.
And I remember one time-- you know, you hear the air raids.
You know, they were bombing the railroads.
And one time, they hit the four corners of the camp.
I guess that was a mark that they knew it was a camp, a concentration camp.
But nobody got hurt.
And one time, we went on the-- they bombed the railroad track.
The railroads-- you know, the cars, whatever they were shipping.
And we used to go down there and clean that up.
And one car load of sugar-- that they were transporting sugar.
And so we were eating sugar like crazy.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Then, then we-- it came-- it came-- orders came that we were going to have to leave from here, too.
So they boxed us in the cars.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And there was another camp.
Muhldorf was not in the Waldlager, it was not in the forest.
There was another camp also, in the same village.
So they boxed us both from both camps.
And boxed us in and we kept on going and going.
And they were going to take us to the Taurus, around the Taurus Mountains, but the train couldn't go through anymore.
So they were going to take us to liquidate us, to kill us over there.
So what they did-- You knew that?
Yes.
Well, we heard that afterwards.
No, we didn't know that at first.
We found that out later on.
So one time they, they'd gone back and forth, back and forth.
The train kept on going.
It seemed like it.
Then one time, the train stops in a-- in a-- in a village.
And they said, everybody out.
We all came out.
And the transport fuhrer who was in charge of the transport said, the war is over, and you are all free to go.
Well, we didn't know what to do, you know?
So better go here, to go there?
So everybody went, you know, in different directions.
Was this the Americans that said, you're free?
No, no, no.
It was still the Germans who said it.
It was the transport fuhrer, the one who was in charge of the transport, said, you're all free to go.
But that wasn't really true.
We were surrounded and they were shooting at us.
And a lot of people-- a lot of people got killed.
A lot of people died-- died and wounded.
And so they took--
Well, I was one of the lucky ones.
I, I just went-- you know, I started running back towards where the train was.
And I went back into the train.
Yeah.
And then they took all--
Yes.
Yes, I had.
Actually, he was from, from Belgium.
Gee, I don't remember.
But I do know he was from Antwerp.
And he said that his, his parents were in the diamond business.
And he said to me-- because I used to help him out.
What I forgot to tell you is when I-- when I got to this camp in Muhldorf-- from the marching from Warsaw to Dachau, my heel from my foot got infected.
And at first, I couldn't go work and carrying the cement.
So they noticed it, so they gave me a job in the camp to clean up the mess from diarrhea.
Everybody had diarrhea.
Excuse me.
And so the one-- the kapo from the camp, he gave me an extra bowl of soup.
Yes.
Yeah.
And then he--
Pardon me?
Well, because, because he wants a-- we did a good job, yes.
And this fellow from Antwerp, Belgium, he was also with us, with me.
And I see-- you know, I see to it that he gets an extra bowl of soup also.
So he told me, he said, my parents were in the diamond business and we are rich people.
We were rich people.
And he says, we get-- we-- when we are going to be free again, you're going to come with me to Belgium.
And well, I'll take care of you.
But we, somehow, we didn't make it together.
I don't know exactly.
Somehow, we got separated along the way.
And I never saw him again.
No.
So I don't know whether he remained alive or not.
So anyway, that, that false liberation.
So I ran back to the boxcar, you know?
And then they took all the dead and wounded, threw them in in one car, and we kept on going again.
We kept on going again.
Then the train stops again.
What's happening now?
What's happening now?
So they said the Red Cross is going to give you guys soup.
We are going to let out one boxcar at a time.
So everybody is waiting for their turn, you know, that they're-- and all of a sudden, there were planes attacking us.
So the people-- Bombs falling?
Yeah.
Not bombs, machine gunning.
It was the American planes.
You see, what happened is that they had an artillery connected to the end of the train.
And they thought it was the soldiers, you know?
It was an army train.
And they were-- so the people who were down below, getting the soup-- so they opened up the boxcars for us, so we can run for cover.
Well, everybody got so confused.
We didn't know what to do.
We would start running.
And so the SS was shooting from down below and the planes were shooting from up above.
And it was, again, a big mess.
A lot of people died.
A lot of people died.
And what I did is I jumped underneath, you know, underneath the train, my boxcar.
And the planes were machine gunning and I can see the rails-- it was hitting the rails, so I pulled on this side.
Then it came from this side, I pulled-- oh my goodness.
This is going to be it.
And I also heard where it was coming from, from up above.
I said, oh, boy.
But luckily, I, I remained-- I remained alive.
